title: Why Your AI Prompts Belong in Git
author: Waiflo Team
excerpt: Prompts are code. They encode business logic, edge-case handling, and output contracts. Treating them as disposable configuration is the root cause of most AI feature regressions.
content:
Every engineering team has a story about a broken AI feature. The LLM output changed, a regression appeared in production, and nobody could pinpoint exactly what had changed — or when. In most cases, the culprit is the same: the prompt lived somewhere outside version control.

Prompts Are Code

A prompt encodes business logic. It tells the model how to behave, what to prioritize, how to format outputs, and what edge cases to handle. When you treat prompts as disposable configuration strings scattered across environment variables, database rows, or comment blocks in a Python file, you lose every property that makes code maintainable:

  • No diffs. You cannot review what changed between the version that worked and the version that broke.
  • No rollbacks. When production breaks, you cannot revert to the last known-good prompt state.
  • No reviews. Prompt changes bypass the pull request process — the same process that catches bugs in every other layer of the system.
  • No attribution. You do not know who changed the prompt, or why.

The Cost of Invisible Changes

Invisible prompt changes are the silent killer of AI reliability. A product manager tweaks a prompt directly in a SaaS dashboard. A developer updates a string constant without thinking. A well-intentioned fix to one output format silently breaks a downstream parser. None of these changes appear in your Git history.

The problem compounds over time. After six months, a prompt has been edited a dozen times, and nobody has a reliable mental model of its current behavior. Testing it feels like guessing.

What Version-Controlled Prompts Look Like

When prompts live in versioned files, you get everything you take for granted in the rest of your codebase. A .waiflo.json file containing your workflow is committed like any other source file:

git diff HEAD~1 workflows/support_triage.waiflo.json
-  "ws_system_prompt": "You are a support agent. Classify tickets.",
+  "ws_system_prompt": "You are a senior support engineer. Classify tickets by severity and category."

This diff is reviewable, attributable, and reversible. Your CI pipeline can run evaluations against it. Your teammates can leave comments. If it breaks something, git revert works in seconds.

Typed Schemas Close the Loop

Version control alone is not enough. Even a perfectly versioned prompt can silently produce outputs that break downstream code if the output format changes. Waiflo's ws_output_schema makes the output contract explicit and machine-readable. When the schema changes, the diff shows it. When the output violates the schema, the runtime catches it.

The combination — versioned prompts with typed schemas — gives you the same level of confidence for AI logic that you have for any other typed, tested module in your system.

Starting Small

You do not need to migrate everything at once. Start with your most critical AI feature: the one where a regression would be most painful. Move its prompt to a .waiflo.json file. Commit it. Add a CI check that runs your evaluation suite against it. The feedback loop you gain in the first week will make the case for doing the same everywhere else.

Your prompts are already doing the work of code. It is time to treat them that way.
